body.text: BLOSSOMS OF HOPE IN TURBULENT TIMES
2023 ANNUAL REPORT
The planet’s greatest, most undercapitalized opportunity
Photo credit: Diego Pérez/SPDA

OUR MISSION: To work in partnership with Indigenous Peoples and local communities to strengthen their tenure and ability to preserve, protect and enjoy the benefits of their traditional lands, territories, and resources.

OUR VISION: Indigenous Peoples and local communities thrive in their ancestral territories with full recognition of their right to own, manage, and develop their traditional lands, territories, and resources.
OUR VALUES: We root our work in three core values: respect, reliability, and trust.
Tenure Facility was established in 2017. It is registered as a non-profit organisation in Sweden with a flexible team presence in countries across Asia, Africa, and Latin America.

Last year Tenure Facility’s partners succeeded in getting governments to formalise indigenous and community land and forest rights to over 2 million hectares and made progress on improving tenure security and governance in almost 22 million hectares, benefiting nearly 14,000 communities.
Behind these big numbers lie thousands of individual stories and successes, trials, and tribulations. You will find some in this main report, and hundreds more in this appendix.
We are well on our way to achieving our ambitious target of improving tenure security and governance in 60 million hectares by 2027 – an area one and a half times the size of California.

In 2023 we gave US$ 26 million to 32 projects in 18 countries. Thanks to our generous donors, we were able to double the number of projects we funded and the amount of onward grants we provided, compared to 2022. That contributed to much greater impact and laid the groundwork for much more to come.

We emphasise tropical rainforests critical to the global climate and biodiversity. Overwhelming scientific evidence shows that indigenous and community rights over and sound stewardship of these areas protect vital natural systems and help to stabilize the climate. Where Indigenous Peoples and other traditional communities manage forests and have strong rights over them, this reduces forest loss, safeguards natural habitats, and slows climate change. By protecting their territories, livelihoods and cultures, these forest guardians are protecting us and the planet.

We take gender equality seriously. We funded 7 major organisations led by women (3 of which are all-women organisations) and 3 projects focus entirely on women’s rights to land and forest. Almost all the projects we funded achieved notable results related to gender with regards to policies, tenure claims, leadership roles, organisational spaces, gender training and awareness, and livelihoods.

Our partners use the support we provide to 1) Push and partner with governments, to get them to officially recognise communal land and forest rights and support indigenous and community efforts to remain in harmony with nature; 2) Renovate and invigorate customary institutions, to protect communities and their natural and cultural riches from external threats, improve their wellbeing, and ensure women’s rights are respected, and youth can find their place; and 3) Strengthen their own organisations, to make them more effective, accountable, and inclusive.

Our policies and practices are tailored-made for our partners. We a) Fund partners to prepare proposals and consult with communities; b) Provide larger and longer grants than most – US$ 500,000 to US$ 1,500,000 a year with five-year grant agreements; c) Allow partners to respond to emerging challenges and opportunities; d) Customise our funding and reporting approaches based on partners’ capacities and contexts; and e) Respect rights and concerns about maps, photos, and stories.

We do not just give money. We provide strategic and technical advice, learning opportunities, and emotional support. Less year we co-sponsored six major regional and global learning exchanges, with 1,500 participants. We help partners get results at scale by facilitating new links to government authorities and other sources of funding, training, and solidarity.

We also help to systematise and share these experiences and work with the Path to Scale coalition, the Global Alliance of Territorial Communities (GATC), and other allies to make the case for greater and better funding for similar efforts.
Nonette’s message
MOVING FORWARD

We are living in turbulent times, and in an increasingly turbulent climate. We are in the throes of wildlife extinction, dwindling forests, run-away climate change, the loss of languages, and loss of the cultures that nurture this natural world.

But as a young person walking the forest path with my father, I learned that Indigenous Peoples, are securing their territories. Despite increasing pressures, they are protecting their forests, peat lands, and waterways – and our climate and the planet’s biodiversity. They can sustain these efforts and can do more. Save more forest. Live better and safer – with law, peaceful negotiation, and practical steps. But they need our help now.
They need more than quick fly-in fly-out cooperation to run the odd workshop. Our partners are wise and need wise collaboration.

That is why I am so excited to see how the Tenure Facility, since our founding seven years ago, is fulfilling its mandate – to dramatically scale and shore up Indigenous and community land and forest guardianship across the world.

2023 was a year of abundance and growth for us. This was thanks to our supportive donors, and the hard work of our partners. Our main partners include most of the major Indigenous Peoples, Afro-descendant, and community organisations that are protecting tropical forests, lands, and waterways across Africa, Asia, and Latin America.

Together we built on last year’s soil preparation, planting, and the favourable seasons. Our strategies became clear. Our systems stronger. More partners got their projects up and running. Many successfully expanded their impact, even with dark clouds on the horizon. We will enter next year with a summer of harvesting and richer results from this year’s growth.

With you, our allies, we helped secure and improve community tenure and governance in 21.7 million hectares of land and forests – three times our progress last year – benefiting almost 14,000 communities.
We are well on the way to meeting our audacious goal of 60 million hectares with secure land and forest tenure by 2027 – an area one and a half times the size of California!

We increased the number of projects we directly support. We have 32 large projects managed and implemented by Indigenous Peoples, Afro-descendant, and community organisations and a handful of allied NGOs. These projects span 18 countries. We exponentially increased Tenure Facility’s onward grant disbursal to projects and organisations. At the same time, we kept our central overhead slim.

The way forward is not straight. I was raised in the forests of the Philippines, where Indigenous Peoples and communities live. Early in my life I saw their territories and forests violently stolen by illegal loggers, miners, plantation companies, and drug dealers. Men, women, and young people died to defend the places they called home. My own friends were affected. As I write, many rural people, who are defending their forests and territories, still suffer death threats, rape, harassment, illegal evictions, and forced assimilation.
But that has not stopped them, and it won’t stop us. We move forward together, with them and with you. Together. For all of us.
NONETTE ROYO
Executive Director Tenure Facility

body.text: INTRODUCTION
The Giving Trees – And The Women Who Protect Them With Their Lives
Photo credit: If Not Us Then Who
We kick off this year’s annual report with two truths, and a story that brings them to life.

TRUTH 1: Your family’s wellbeing depends on whether humanity can summon the will to protect the lungs of the planet, our last remaining intact forests. If devilmay-care destruction continues to trounce the better angels of our nature, our last intact forest ecosystems will collapse. As a direct consequence, we will lose the ability to meet our international climate goals, drive millions of plant and animal species into extinction, and experience health-and-safety threats at a scale no government is prepared to address.

TRUTH 2: You have much more ability to prevent this scenario than you realise. You don’t have to despair. Simply listen to, learn from, and support the most courageous and knowledgeable forest guardians on the planet: the indigenous, Afro-descendant, and other traditional local communities living in tropical forests. They are already successfully protecting their ancestral territories, lands and waterways They are conserving wildlife – and protecting our climate. Read on to learn how and why they matter, and how having secure tenure over their land, forests and waterways makes a difference.
Now for the story that connects both truths.
BABASSU PALM FORESTS

(Attalea speciosa) cover 200,000 square kilometres across the Amazon Basin extending from Brazil to Bolivia, Peru, Guyana, and Suriname. They do not come immediately to mind when imagining the deep rainforest. But they are home to an impressive explosion of life, including macaws, parrots, monkeys, armadillos, anteaters, giant otters, river dolphins, and geckos. The palm absorbs carbon and funnels rainwater down its fibrous trunk, storing both underground, and providing moisture to the surrounding vegetation.

The oil from the babassu palm nut is rich in anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial, and hydrating properties. The nuts provide a sustainable livelihood for hundreds of thousands of mostly Afro-descendant women in northern Brazil. After collecting the nuts, the women – known as nut breakers or quebradeiras – crack the nuts apart with small axes while sitting and singing together in a communal circle. Later, all the women share the profits of their labour. Like forest guardians all over the world, they protect the health of the babassu as if their lives depend on it. Because it does.
They are warrior women.

Four decades ago, cattle ranchers stopped the women from collecting the nuts. They burnt down and poisoned the palms to establish pasture. Some women paid a terrible price when they went to collect nuts – murder, rape, and the torching of their small wooden houses.

In response the women organised themselves into what is now one of the largest rural women’s movements in the world, the Inter-State Movement of Babassu Women Nut Breakers (MIQCB), with more than 300,000 women.

The Babassu women approached the Tenure Facility for support to expand Free Babassu Laws across more municipalities, enforce existing Laws, and get title to their forest lands; and in 2023 we gave them their first grant. We also started funding the National Coordination of Rural Black Quilombola Communities. Quilombolas are the descendants of people who escaped slavery by taking refuge in the forests. These two partners are working closely with national and local government agencies to secure legal title to 200 territories and protect millions more hectares and get proper government enforcement of the laws. With Tenure Facility support, they hope to leverage our funding to get additional finance from the Brazilian Bank for National Development (BNDES).

children.text: They persuaded 17 municipalities to pass Free Babassu Laws, protecting the babassu forests and giving the women quebradeiras full access to them. “Our struggle for rights is directly related to broader environmental goals,” said Maria Alaídes Alves de Souza, a coordinator of the MIQCB. “The whole world could learn from our relationship with nature. Protecting the forest means protecting food security, water, air, life for all of us. There are ways to live while protecting the forest and biodiversity. We, the traditional peoples, and communities, do that.”

It is a golden moment. The new Brazilian Ministry of Racial Equality is enthusiastic about issuing title to Quilombola lands. Brazil’s president, Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, is eager to showcase his global leadership on forests and climate change in the run-up to the 2025 climate conference (COP30) that Brazil will host.

body.text: The Trees Signposting Our Forest Path
We start off this report by delving deep into our Why? – the moral, ethical, climate and ecological imperatives and the scientific data that drive our passion and focus.

In Section 2, we turn to the Who? – we, the Tenure Facility, our evolution and what makes us unique. And our partners in Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Who are they? The “Davids” and “Davidas” achieving improbable victories on the forest frontlines against global Goliaths.

In Section 5, we explain the How – in other words, our “special sauce” – with an emphasis on how we put our values into practice on the ground, in our grant-making and our operations. This section will not be a boring technical manual or cheery public relations brochure. We share realistically how are our internal systems and governance are working, whether they are fit-for-purpose, and what we see as ongoing challenges.
In Section 3, What Did We Do? and Where? – the projects we funded, in which countries, and where we supported new projects.

children.text: In the final Section 7, we look to What Next? – what’s next for the Tenure Facility and our partners in 2024, our aspirations and inspirations.
And for those who might still want more insights, you can refer to several data-rich appendices with detailed results, project and partners summaries, and media stories about our work.

body.text: In Section 4, What We Achieved? – what results our partners got from their projects, and what they did to make change happen. We describe the strategies they used, what’s working, what’s not, the challenges they faced and how they coursecorrected. We also talk about our own efforts to support learning, new alliances, and the education of donors about these issues.
In Section 6, we describe the With What? – our board of directors, our team, our donors, and our financials.
SECTION 1:
Why?

Over a thirda of the world’s healthiest tropical rainforests are within Indigenous Peoples’ lands. More generally, of all global lands that are still in good ecological condition, at least 42 percent are managed by Indigenous Peoples and traditional communities. These remaining forest lands, with their waterways, peatlands, and wildlife, play an essential role in supporting all life on Earth.

About one billion rural people in the tropics depend on forests to make ends meetc. Their diversity of languages and cultures are rooted in these sacred places. Their territories “contain 80 percent of the world’s remaining biodiversity and intersect about 40 percent of all terrestrial protected areas and ecologically intact landscapesd.”

Where Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities have secure rights and tenure over their territories, deforestation is lower, forests are healthier, fires are fewer – and the planet is better offe. But currently they have legal rights over only 40 percent of the landsf they manage.
Given how important forests are, the large share of forests

children.text: BOX 1: WHY FORESTS MATTER

RIGHTLY CALLED the lungs of the planet, tropical forests have a largescale impact on the climate. While reducing fossil fuel emissions is essential for mitigating climate change, so is protecting and restoring forests.

They suck up water and pump it into the air around them – a process called transpiration – creating big water vapour “rivers in the sky” that generate rainfall for farmers in places hundreds of miles away.
As forests shrink, these vital functions are rapidly dwindling. The planet is on a breakneck roller coaster ride towards “tipping points’ beyond which climate will spiral out of control.

Forests capture carbon pollution on a hugeb scale absorbing it from the atmosphere through photosynthesis from trillions of leaves on billions of trees, producing oxygen, and then storing the carbon in their tree trunks and soils. Trees also provide extra cooling for the planet – like natural air conditioners.
Protecting forests can help slow this run-away train while we transition away from fossil fuels.

Indigenous Peoples and communities manage, the proven efficacy of their stewardship, and the low cost of securing their land rights, one might expect that a lot is being invested in that. In fact, only a minuscule amount of international funding has gone for that purpose – on average only US$ 270 million globally per year between 2011 and 2020; less than 1 percent of international climate aidg.)

body.text: To have any hope of saving the last intact forest ecosystems and the cultures and peoples and wildlife that protect them, we must empower forest guardians, help them secure and sustain their tenure over their lands and forests. And support them now.

children.text: BOX 2: WHAT IS LAND AND FOREST TENURE?

Tenure is the way by which communities hold property rights to land and resources such as forests and waterways. These rights may be conferred by rules stemming from custom and common use, as well as formal laws.

items.text: Demonstrate that their rights to land and forests are recognised by law or custom.
Protect and sustainably use resources from their land, free from imposition or dispute from others.

children.text: Secure tenure can take many forms, but they all provide Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities the ability to:
• Have confidence their rights and relationship to their lands and forests are safe, enforced, and accepted as legitimate.

body.text: Overwhelming Evidence That Indigenous Peoples And
Community Forest Management 2 Mowat, H. and Veit, P.G. The
Are Good For Forests, People, And The Climate
The scientific community has spoken. Where Indigenous Peoples and other traditional communities manage forests and have strong rights over them, forest loss is reduced and climate change mitigated.

One World Resources Institute (WRI) study found that deforestation rates were two to three times lower in titled indigenous territories than in other Amazon forest lands in Brazil, Bolivia, and Colombia. They estimated the carbon mitigation benefits of the titled territories would be US$ 25 billion – US$ 34 billion over 20 years.1

Then the Inter-Government Panel on Climate Change (IPCC)’s first report on land and climate change in 2019 recognised the importance of securing community land for climate change.2 At least three subsequent UN reviews have reaffirmed that.3
Another research team demonstrated that across the tropics, indigenous territories reduce forest destruction as much or more than in protected areas, despite receiving much less funding.4

Project Drawdown calculated that by doubling the area where Indigenous Peoples have secure legal rights to forests could reduce global carbon emissions by 8.69 Gigatons of CO2 equivalent by 2050. That equals about one fifth of the world’s annual CO2 emissions.

2023 witnessed a virtual explosion of similar reports, with more than 20 peer-reviewed studies pointing in the same direction. A global review of 320 econometric studies found that Indigenous Peoples and community forest management were two of the variables most consistently associated with less deforestation.5 Another study of over 300 sites in 15 countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America showed that where rural communities have formal control and practical influence over forests, the forests store more carbon, have greater biodiversity, and provide better livelihood outcomes.6
Among eight 2023 studies from South America’s Amazon Basin and Chaco region, six highlight lower deforestation in titled indigenous territories and two
IPCC Calls for Community Land Rights to Fight Climate Change, World Resources Institute (WRI) (2019). https://www.wri.org/insights/ ipcc-calls-securing-communityland-rights-fight-climate-change.

greater forest regrowth.7 One study notes that while most of the Amazon Basin already emits more carbon than it absorbs, the indigenous and Afro-descendant territories remain major carbon sinks. In fact, indigenous territories in the Amazon soak up as much carbon each year as, for comparison, the UK spews out from fossil fuels.8

There are also proven social benefits. Protecting indigenous territories in the Brazilian Amazon helped avoid 15 million cases of respiratory and cardiovascular diseases each year and saved about US$ 2 billion dollars annually in health costs.9 Where communities had secure forest tenure, they also had more cooperative behaviour, higher levels of trust and stronger organisations.10 Community forest tenure leads to sustainable poverty alleviation11, as found in programmes yielding positive livelihood benefits in Ethiopia12 and India.13 In Peru, communal tenure performed better for ensuring poor families got access to the forest products they depend on.14

Three studies from Nepal link the rise of community forestry with reduced deforestation and increased tree cover.15 Participating communities plant more trees, patrol their forests, and restrict grazing there.
Indigenous territories even do a much better job of protecting forests in the numerous biodiversity hotspots around the world plagued by armed conflicts.16

body.text: Photo credit: Jackie Lebo/Content House Kenya/Tenure Facility
Two Contradictory Trends And High Stakes For The Outcome
While there were many dark clouds on the horizon in 2023 and real cause for alarm, there were also positive signs. Which prevail, will depend on all of us. And the stakes could not be higher.

One the downside, Latin America’s Indigenous and Afro-descendant Peoples, communities, and forests faced a litany of threats from ranchers, gold miners, oil companies, soya bean farmers, loggers, drug traffickers, fires, and infrastructure projects. Even some communities with land titles suffered land invasions, fires from nearby farms, or mining or logging concessions imposed without consent. Progress in Africa was stymied by large new oil, mining, and oil palm deals, Jihadist violence and coups, and armed militias in the Congo. In Asia increasingly authoritarian national governments prioritised large-scale coal, minerals, and oil projects, oil palm and pulp plantations, and logging over social or environmental concerns, and cracked down on groups who dissented.

The United States and Europe were distracted by horrific wars in Ukraine and Gaza, political uncertainty, and high inflation. Parts of Europe experienced a backlash to domestic climate policies as consumers and farmers fretted about how these might affect their pocketbooks.
Meanwhile, the global climate crisis showed troublesome signs of spiralling out of control (record heatwaves, fires, floods).

On the upside, deforestation rates plummeted in Brazil and Colombia after respective presidents Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and Gustavo Petro enacted positive forest and land policies. Suriname’s parliament debated the country’s first law enshrining Indigenous and Tribal Peoples’ land rights. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) implemented favourable policies such as local community forest concessions and an Indigenous Peoples’ Rights Law. Kenya and Liberia slowly moved ahead on recognising communal land rights. Asia saw an uptick in government recognition of customary forests in Indonesia and Forest Rights Act implementation in a few states in India, as well as an on-going commitment to community forests in Nepal.

Within the mixed panorama on climate action, the relatively narrow objective of securing community land and forest tenure – a proven and cost-effective approach with outsized benefits – was a source of inspiration.

The global five-year US$ 1.7 billion Forest Tenure Pledge, signed by donors in late 2021 at the climate conference (COP26) in Glasgow, continued to resonate and highlight the need to recognise and reward Indigenous Peoples and communities for their role as nature’s guardians. Donors who signed the pledge and others boosted their funding.

Public interest and awareness grew as the world began to wake up to the opportunities for tapping into the courage, leadership, and knowledge of the likes of the women babassu nut breakers and the tens of millions of other forest guardians around the globe.
SECTION 2:
Who?
What makes the Tenure Facility unique?

We specialise in funding major Indigenous Peoples (IP), Afrodescendant (AFD) and traditional community organisations in the pan-tropics, with our operating systems specifically designed to respect their rights and meet their needs. Tenure Facility is the only large international financial mechanism dedicated to supporting community land and forest rights and their governance.
We are the largest funder of these organisations. The size of our grants – US$ 500,000 to US$ 1.5 million a year – makes a significant difference on the ground.
We also enter long-term partnerships of five years or more, providing continuity so community organisations can ramp up their efforts in forest protection and get on a firm footing.
But we do not just give money: we provide strategic and technical advice, learning opportunities, and emotional support.

We help IP, AFD, and traditional community organisations to connect to governments, and to other sources of funding, training, and solidarity. Helping our partners develop strong collaborations with others helps them get results at scale.

We pay special attention to gender equality and youth inclusion, and to cultural sensitivities. The community organisations with which partner are diverse, as are their leaderships; they involve the old and young, women, and minorities.

At the Tenure Facility, we also work at the global level with partners and allies to get key donors and intermediaries to scale up their support for IP, AFD, and other community organisations, in ways that meet their needs.

children.text: BOX 3: GETTING OUR HOUSE IN ORDER

In 2023, we doubled the amount granted to our indigenous and community organisation partners and their projects. Some 75 percent of our total spend reached their hands, and our overhead remained slim at 10 percent.

been working protect integrated forest and water ecosystems – “lifescapes” – but face threats from industrial fishing and tourism. When a new global Marine tenure initiative emerged, we offered a home, agreeing to be their fiscal sponsor in 2024.

U.S. and European donors will find it easier to support us now that we have established a sister entity, the Tenure Facility Fund, with charitable 501 (3) (c) status in the United States, which will help us channel funds.

We continued to decentralise our team – a third of which is now in the tropics. This allowed us to be closer to partners, bringing granular knowledge about diverse cultures and complex political economies.

body.text: MORE THAN TARGETS, MAKING CHANGE HAPPEN
By 2027, we are committed to achieve:

items.text: 15 million hectares of communal land and forest rights with formal government recognition.
60 million hectares with progress towards secure tenure and better territorial governance.
15 million people in indigenous and rural communities benefitting from these advances.

body.text: But it is not just about targets. It is about our five-pronged approach for delivering on our promises.

children.text: BOX 4: FIVE STEPS ALONG OUR PATH TO SCALE
Tenure Facility funds and supports indigenous and community organisations and their allies to:
communities properly, and prepare studies required to process land claims.

Communities can do a lot to strengthen their tenure security and governance without involving government. We support that too. To start, they can bolster their community and indigenous authorities and governance bodies. These may be village assemblies, territorial governments, conservation co-management bodies, women and youth commissions, chiefs, caciques, capitanes, or apus. This often means creating or updating their bylaws and rules for sustainable land use or care of sacred sites, mustering volunteers for communal activities or getting legal or tax status for village enterprises.

items.text: Push governments
Partner with governments
Protect their territories and assert their rights themselves
Strengthen their own capacity
Share lessons and reach out to influence others

children.text: Our funding and support help indigenous and community organisations to both push and partner with governments.

Communities can also strengthen control over lands by participatory mapping, preparing community land use and conservation plans, documenting local knowledge and passing it from one generation to the next, monitoring forests for fires and illegal miners and loggers, resolving conflicts informally, and agreeing on boundaries with neighbours.

Pushing or pressing governments can consist of anything from calling on them to design or implement policies, regulations, and procedures (without violating U.S. lobbying restrictions), taking them to court, or simply helping communities to prepare claims and applications. In most places we work, communities must jump through and over many hoops and hurdles to get land tenure or forest certificates approved. They face myriad, often inconsistent, rules and procedures, involving multiple agencies, and unfamiliar languages or formats. To get anything done in such contexts, takes a little pushing.
For the organisations we fund to do all these things, they also must be strong and well-managed. So, that is another central pillar of our work.
Finally, it is also about sharing lessons and outreach to influence governments, funders, NGOs, and business, and getting more of them actively supporting communal land and forest tenure.

At the same time all the groups we support partner with governments and help them to deliver. They participate in advisory bodies and commissions, assist officials to visit the communities, educate them, help agencies consult
This is the path to scale.
BOX 5: 17 MILLION INDONESIAN VOICES
The Dayak Iban Peoples of Sungai Utik, Indonesia, warmly welcome respectful outsiders who want to visit their long house and learn about their forests and rivers.

Apai traces his community’s struggle for legal tenure back to 1998, when Indonesia’s military dictator, Suharto, was forced to step down. Marginalised communities started to make their voices heard, and Sungai Utik began mapping its territory. Apai and Sungai Utik’s other elders at first got little help from local, provincial, or national officials in navigating the byzantine processes of government agencies.

If you visit, we dare you to try keep up with 80-something elder Appai Janggut on a walk through what he calls “the supermarket” – the pristine hardwood forest that has provided almost all his community’s nutritional and medical needs for centuries. Apai walks through dense forest with an ease unfathomable in a Westerner of a comparable age. He also demonstrates an understanding of forest species that no Western scientist can match – from a potent anti-malarial called engkerebai to a towering Dipterocarpus tree that provides a home for bees to make a honey especially rich in antioxidants and vitamins.

Joining their voices to a movement of indigenous communities across Indonesia, this first cry moved Gus Dur, a new Indonesian president, to recognise the emergent indigenous People’s Alliance of the Archipelago (AMAN). Twenty-six years later, AMAN has become a huge grassroots force and one of Tenure Facility’s largest partners. By amplifying the voices of 17 million indigenous Indonesians, it is helping indigenous or “adat” communities all over the country to take back control of their forestlands.

“Our peoples see the forest as our mother, the river as our father,” Apai explains. “They provide us with our food, medicine, clean water, resins for waterproofing canoes, wood for homes. We have everything we need in these sacred groves.

body.text: WHO ARE OUR PARTNERS?

Tenure Facility has partners in important tropical forests across the world. Most are large indigenous federations and grassroots community-based organisations and networks, whose members, and their cultures and ways of living, are rooted in the enchanting locales they inhabit. A core part of their mission is to defend rights, keep forests standing, and generate sustainable livelihoods.

In 2023, more than three quarters of Tenure Facility projects were managed by a main partner that is a major grassroots indigenous, Afro-descendant, or some other rural community organisation or network. They are sometimes located at the local level, sometimes national, sometimes both. This level of support for these types of organisations is practically unprecedented.
See appendix for summaries of Tenure Facility’s main partners.

Many Indigenous Peoples in the tropics are organised at multiple levels, from local village organisations to national umbrella organisations to broader networks with presence on the global stage. In general, there is a strong thread of legitimacy and accountability of leaders to their memberships: villages gather in assemblies to elect, or select by consensus, their village leaders, who then elect the leaders of the federations at district or provincial level. These, in turn, elect representatives to national and regional organisations, which come together in international coalitions.

children.text: BOX 6: HOW FAR CAN THE VOICE OF A YOUNG HARAKBUT WOMAN FROM A REMOTE FOREST REACH?

Consider a young indigenous Harakbut woman leader in the village of Puerto Luz in the uplands of Madre de Dios in Peru. She is elected to represent her community in the Harakbut, Yine, and Mastigenka Council (COHARYIMA), a regional body which represents 17 villages from three different indigenous ethnicities.

AIDESEP is an active member of the ninecountry Coordinating Body of Indigenous Peoples of the Amazon Basin (COICA), and COICA belongs to the Global Alliance of Territorial Communities (GATC), comprised of indigenous and community organisations from the Amazon and Congo Basins, Mesoamerica, and Indonesia.

COHARYIMA, for its part, belongs to a Tenure Facility partner called the Native Federation of Madre de Dios and Tributaries (FENAMAD). This in turn, forms part of the Interethnic Association for the Development of the Peruvian Rainforest (AIDESEP), which represents all the Indigenous Peoples of the Peruvian Amazon.

Thus, the young Harakbut woman and the issues facing her village are given voice in the Madre de Dios regional capital of Puerto Maldonado, in the Peruvian legislature in Lima, in Belém at a summit of Amazonian Indigenous Peoples, and in New York, Rome or Jakarta at a UN conference.

body.text: Tenure Facility partners with Indigenous Peoples and community organisations at various levels of this vertically integrated chain. FENAMAD in the Peruvian Amazon, for example, represents two federations and 38 communities, which together cover millions of hectares of forests. AMAN in Indonesia, on the other hand, represents 2,332 communities and almost 17 million people across the archipelago. The Coordination of Indigenous Organisations of the Brazilian Amazon (COIAB) in Brazil, National Organization of Indigenous Peoples of the Colombian Amazon (OPIAC) in Colombia, and the Confederation of Indigenous Nationalities of the Ecuadorian Amazon (CONFENAIE) in Ecuador represent all the indigenous communities of the Brazilian, Colombian, and Ecuadorian Amazons, respectively. And CONAIE and COONAPIP represent all the indigenous communities of Ecuador and Panama.

Afro-descendant partners in Latin America are organised alowng the same lines. Malungu represents more than 500 Quilombola communities in the Brazilian state of Para, and in turn is part of CONAQ, the National Coordination Body of Quilombolas, which represents 23 state associations and dozens of other regional and local associations nationwide. Similarly, PCN in Colombia is composed of 140 Afro-Colombian community councils and other grassroots organisations spread across the country, and 77 Saamaka Maroon Tribal villages belong to VSG in Suriname.

We also support specialised units created by these large federations, such as the Podáali, Babassu and Mizizi Dudu territorial community funds in Brazil, Indonesia’s specialised community mapping unit, the Ancestral Domain Registration Agency (BRWA), and Colombia’s indigenous Technical Secretariat (STI-CNTI), designed specifically to negotiate with government.

Some indigenous partners have constituted themselves as NGOs. These vary from Amerindian Peoples Association (APA) in Guyana, with members in over 80 indigenous villages, and John Cho Society (JCS) in Belize, linked to an association of traditional village heads (“alcaldes”), to Union for the Emancipation of Indigenous Women (UEFA) in the DRC, composed of indigenous gender experts who work with local NGOs and women’s groups.

Mixed networks like Community Land Action Now! (CLAN!) in Kenya, TenForest in Burkina Faso, CREF Network in the DRC, and FOKER in Papua each bring together several dozen indigenous and community groups and local NGOs. While these are not massive federations, by coming together as networks they have achieved economies of scale and can manage larger projects.
Some of our partners focus primarily on forest management or farming. FECOFUN in Nepal is comprised of 22,226 community forest user groups throughout the country.

Meanwhile, MIQCB in northern Brazil represents over 300,000 predominantly Afro-descendant women babassu palm nut breakers. KPA Indonesia is an Indonesian federation representing 508 small farmer groups. And CNOP, in Mali, is the largest small farmer organisation in the Sahel.
Of these, ACOFOP in Peten, Guatemala, probably has the greatest technical capacity. They represent 24 community organisations in the Maya Biosphere Reserve in Peten.

children.text: BOX 7: VOICES FROM THE FIELD
A Q&A with Thakur Prasad Bhandari, Chairperson, Federation of Community Forest Users Nepal (FECOFUN)
Q: HOW WOULD YOU SUMMA- RISE THE IMPACT OF FECOFUN ON THE FORESTS OF NEPAL?
Q: WHY HAVE YOU DEVOTED YOUR LIFE TO THE CAUSE OF FORESTRY USERS?

A: FECOFUN has been a driving force promoting and expanding the community forestry model in Nepal. Through advocacy, capacity building, and networking efforts, FECOFUN has helped establish community forest user groups (CFUGs) and has initiated a groundbreaking effort to achieve 50 percent women’s involvement in community forestry in Nepal.

A: It has always been painful to me that women, Dalits, Indigenous Peoples, and other marginalised people who have devoted their whole lives to forest conservation and management have been denied their rightful access to forest resources. From childhood, I knew I would devote my life to the cause of forestry users, thinking they must exercise their full rights over forests and land.
Q: PLEASE DESCRIBE THE MAIN CHALLENGES FOR FORESTRY USERS IN NEPAL.

A: Indigenous peoples and local communities lack full security over forests and land resources and face legal hurdles to exercising their rights to land, territories, and natural resources. This has been a major impediment in improving their livelihoods through community-based forest enterprises.
THAKUR PRASAD BHANDARI,
Chairperson, Federation of Community Forest Users Nepal (FECOFUN)

We have now federated more than 22,000 CFUGs into FECOFUN. These CFUGs cover 3.1 million households and protect more than 2.3 million hectares of forests. That’s 37 percent of Nepal’s total forest area.

BOX 8: MORE RESPLENDENT THAN THE QUETZAL’S PLUMAGE

body.text: Although most of the main partners in our projects are indigenous, Afro-descendant, or community organisations, that is not always the case. Sometimes we see opportunities to advance land rights where there are no strong indigenous or community organisations except a few tiny village clusters or groups at local level, or areas where governments restrict our ability to fund communities. Then we seek other types of network or NGO to fund and help smaller community organisations grow.

This includes some of our partners in the DRC, Liberia, and India. We support two national NGOs in the DRC – CAGDFT and Tropenbos DRC – to help develop community forest concessions and simple forest plans, and to promote rights-based approaches to conservation.
In Liberia, our main partner, Parley, is a national NGO which has been heavily involved in community land rights and forest management work since the end of the country’s civil war.

In India, where the government restricts foreign funding for most grassroots organisations, we have opted to work with one of the country’s elite private business schools (ISB) and two national NGOs (SRUTI and Vasundhara.

Many projects we fund also involve other organisations who play more limited, typically financial, or technical, roles. Some are fiscal sponsors, others produce studies and/or provide legal assistance or communications or mapping support, and/or different types of training.

SECTION 4:
What Did We Achieve?
A lot with a little.

How many times have you heard people say that we need billions of dollars to solve this or that major world challenge? Maybe we do, but it is amazing what people can achieve with a tiny fraction of that when they are well-organised, highly motivated, and understand the problems.

Relatively small amounts of money can go a long way when you have the sort of partners Tenure Facility works with. These are people who work long hours, often volunteer their time, and are willing to put their lives on the line to protect their families and ancestral lands.

In 2023, they achieved more with the US$ 26 million that the Tenure Facility channelled to their organisations than many big multilateral or government projects that spend many times more could even dream of.

Over the year, these partner organisations were able to get governments in nine different countries to formally recognise the rights of 1,033 communities over more than two million hectares of land and forests. Hundreds of thousands of people living in villages in those areas can sleep easier now, knowing their lands and futures are more secure.

But that was just the tip of the iceberg. In total, 13,896 communities were able to make some sort of measurable progress towards being able to protect the places they call home, preserve their cultures, and manage their affairs in an area almost the size of the United Kingdom – 21.7 million hectares.
This represented more than double the number of hectares and communities that got formal recognition and made other types of progress in 2022.

This left us well positioned to achieve our three ambitious targets for 2027: (15 million hectares of formal recognition, 60 million hectares in total with meaningful progress, and 15 million people who benefit).

body.text: Policy Reforms Favouring Communal Land Rights

When the Rights and Resources Initiative NGO helped to establish Tenure Facility in 2018, it assumed we would only fund projects to implement government policies in countries that already had favourable laws and procedures. But government policies and the resolve to implement them fluctuate, meaning there are always gaps and contradictions. So, many partners have had to use our projects to overcome policy bottlenecks to move ahead.

Six rounds of negotiations between Colombia’s main indigenous organisations – represented by CNTI, the Indigenous Technical Secretariat of National Commission of Indigenous Territories – and government land agencies and government agencies led to 93 agreements about budgets and procedures, and expediting urgent cases.

Meanwhile, the Proceso De Comunidades Negras, representing Afro-Colombian communities, was the driving force behind new regulations related to land titling and mining, logging, and water use their territories, some signed by the president himself, before a huge crowd.

Colombia’s new National Development Plan mandated the creation of community forest concessions in protected forest reserves, based largely on learning exchanges between a variety of Colombian groups and the community forest concessions represented by ACOFOP in Guatemala’s Maya Biosphere Reserve.

In Peru, public awareness efforts by FENAMAD, ORPIO, and SPDA about the need to protect uncontacted and recently contacted Indigenous Peoples helped to convince the National Congress to shelve a legislative initiative that threatened the country’s four million hectares of reserves for these vulnerable groups and the forests they inhabit.

children.text: CAGDFT collaborated with other NGOs and government agencies in the DRC to inform a new Land Use Planning Law approved by the National Assembly. The Law gives Indigenous Peoples and other local communities a stronger voice in land use zoning and recognises their right to reject harmful investment projects them and be compensated in advance if they are evicted.

body.text: RECOGNITION OF LAND AND FOREST RIGHTS
Usually when one thinks about communal land and forest rights the first thing that comes to mind is a land title. But formal recognition of these rights can take various forms.
While there was progress on this front in nine countries we worked in, our partners’ most notable results were in Bolivia, Indonesia, Nepal, and India.

While Bolivia and Indonesia accounted for three-quarters of the 2,048,000 hectares that governments recognised during the year, India and Nepal accounted for 87 percent of the 1,033 communities that benefitted.
In Bolivia, the government formally recognised two autonomous indigenous governments as the official authorities in two large lowland territories.

children.text: BOX 9: MARCHING FOR TERRITORY AND DIGNITY IN THE BOLIVIAN AMAZON

on an arduous walk to claim “dignity and respect for territory”. It took the Amazon community “marchistas” 34 days to reach the freezing Andean Mountain capital city of La Paz, with many groups joining them as they winded their way up hill along dirt trails far from their tropical forest home.

Indigenous Peoples in two territories of lowland Bolivia had their traditional authorities recognised as autonomous governments responsible for governing and conserving huge areas of lands, forests, and wetlands. They used their project resources to elect and train their authorities (men and women), develop new territorial governance and conservation laws, and verify demarcation of additional areas within their territorial boundaries.

Some distance away in the Cavineña Nation Assembly there were “jidadya, jidadya, jidadya!” shouts of joy as Bolivia’s Constitutional Court recognised traditional government as an autonomous indigenous territorial unit. It covered 473,480 hectares, including 1,618 additional hectares that were identified during the verification of the territory’s. In coordination with the Vice Ministry of Autonomy, the Cavineños community used project resources to help advance the process recognising the new autonomous government and recover the additional territory.

In July, more than a thousand men and women from the five Peoples – Mojeño Ignaciano, Mojeño Trinitario, Tsimane, Yuracare, and Movima – gathered in a forest clearing in the indigenous Multiethnic Territory to cast their secret vote. They placed their ballot papers in baskets woven from palm leaves – traditional urns. The process was accredited by the Bolivian government. Three of the five leaders elected by the communities were women. This newly recognised autonomous government now has formal responsibility for an area of 650,125 hectares, half of which includes the Loma Santa Conservation Area where communities protect important riverheads and wildlife from illegal mining and fires.

The communities remembered their history with emotion as they took back their territory which had been taken away from them by missionaries, then by the army in the 1900s. But it now faces new illegal incursions by big cattle ranchers and illegal fishers.
It was from this territory over 30 years ago that the first “Indigenous March” set out

The Kaxuyana, Tunayana, Kayana, and other Indigenous Peoples have inhabited an area in the middle of that territory for hundreds, if not thousands, of years. That area is now called the Kaxuyana-Tunayana Indigenous Territory , or TIKT, and is the only remaining piece of the massive Wayamu Territoriy still to be demarcated. In 1968, the Brazilian government forcibly removed most of the Kaxuyanas; dropping them in an unfamiliar savanna area near the Suriname border. After 30 years in exile, many managed to return in the 1990s. Since 2002, they have fought for government recognition of their territory, for them, seven other Indigenous Peoples, and several isolated indigenous groups that remain uncontacted.

Even then, efforts to physically demarcate the territory did not get underway until last year, when Luis Ignacio Lula de Silva became president. To kick off the process, FUNAI, COIAB, the Podáali Fund, local indigenous groups and others held a three-day planning workshop. COIAB negotiated with FUNAI about how this would work and helped to coordinate the groups. Podáali funded local indigenous groups to strengthen their organisations, participate actively in the demarcation, and monitor illegal mining and logging. They expect the process to conclude in 2024.
The process has been torturously slow. The government agency responsible

body.text: In India, the State Governments of Odisha and Jharkhand dramatically ramped up efforts to implement the country’s 2006 Forest Rights Act. Our partners Vasundhara, the Indian School of Business (ISB), and the Society for Rural Urban and Tribal Initiative (SRUTI) helped almost 10,000 villages in Odisha to make progress towards obtaining rights over 882,645 hectares.

children.text: BOX 11: TRIBAL FOREST-DWELLERS OBTAIN RIGHTS ACROSS ODISHA, INDIA
leaders. The State Government entrusted our partner Vasundhara, an Odisha-based NGO, to coordinate the scheme in half the State’s 30 districts.

When India’s parliament approved the Forest Rights Act in 2006 experts estimated it could help over 150 million tribal forest dwellers in 170,000 villages to obtain rights to 40 million hectares of forestlands (an area larger than Germany). However, only a fraction of that has been achieved. Most State Governments charged with implementing the Act have been unwilling or unable to do so.

It was a truly massive effort. Vasundhara hired 52 programme officers, trainers, and field assistants, and recruited 1,018 local community volunteers to help file claims at the village levels. In a matter of months, they developed a web platform for claims in 15 districts, conducted “awareness camps” in 9,487 villages in 11 districts, trained 1,349 community volunteers and public officials, and helped 645 villages to file Community Forest Rights claims. In total, they managed to reach over 9,815 villages covering 882,645 hectares; and by the end of the year 328 communities that had submitted claims during earlier project phases received Community Forest Right certificates to almost 70,000 hectares. ISB and SRUTI complemented these efforts.

Last year Odisha was the big exception. With an eye towards the next election, in a State where tribal peoples make up over one fifth of the electorate, a highprofile scheme called “My Forestland” was launched to recognise the rights of 32,000 communities in just two years.
The new scheme came with its own programme management unit with a healthy budget, awareness campaigns, teams in every district, and extensive training of public officials and village

body.text: Thanks to strong support from President Gustavo Petro and Vice President Francia Marquez and recent efforts to strengthen our partners CNTI and PCN, there was a notable uptick in processing Afro-Colombian and indigenous land claims, with over 70,000 hectares titled.

Guatemala not only completed the renewal of the community forest concessions in the Peten Maya Biosphere Reserve in a ceremony presided over by then President Alejandro Giammattei, but it also approved two new community forest concessions covering 62,373 hectares.
CUSTOMARY GOVERNANCE AUTHORITIES

For communal land and forest rights to reap their main social and environmental benefits, the customary authorities that manage the resources must be well-organised, accountable, and inclusive, and have clear rules and responsibilities. So, strengthening those authorities is a big part of most projects we fund.
These authorities go by a wide variety of names. But the essence is always the same. Residents volunteer their time and get elected to serve and protect their neighbours, lands, water, and forests.

Last year our partners helped thousands of these local groups to prepare statutes and constitutions, hold assemblies and elections, gain formal recognition, make strategic and operational plans, train village leaders about local laws and how to perform their duties, set rules about natural resources, and implement small projects.

In response to the growing exodus of rural youth, partners in at least 10 countries took steps to engage them. They held workshops, dialogues, and leadership conferences, ensured youth were represented in local decision-making bodies, and provided training, seed money, and access to land for small livelihood, cultural, and natural resource projects.
CONFLICT RESOLUTION

These customary authorities and the partner organisations that represent them play vital roles in resolving conflicts over land and other resources. Thirteen projects report notable results in this regard.
Our partners helped to address these conflicts at multiple levels.
The KPA Consortium for Agrarian Reform in Indonesia and CNTI in Colombia took a macro approach, regularly monitoring major land conflicts and working with government authorities to resolve them.

KPA works largely to resolve conflicts between the hundreds of communities they represent and the large palm oil, pulp, and timber plantations. Each year they publish a national report on land conflicts and partner with the Ministry of Agrarian Affairs to solve the most urgent ones. Last year they jointly prioritised 30 conflict areas one which to focus.

In Colombia, CNTI and PCN worked closely with the government to resolve major land disputes. The Ministry of Agriculture, the National Land Agency and CNTI jointly developed an inter-institutional protocol for addressing different types of conflicts. PCN was key in the Inter-Ethnic Forum of Cauca established by Vice President Francia Marquez and local governments to resolve violent land disputes. These collaborations allowed CNTI to help the Pasos and Nukak Maku Indigenous Peoples regain partial control over their titled territory covering almost one million hectares in Guaviare, and to get the government to provide protection to four Sikuani indigenous communities seeking title to 89,998 hectares in Meta.

At the more local level, at least 10 projects were heavily involved in efforts to harmonise boundaries and resolve dozens of long-standing disputes between neighbouring villages and/or ethnic groups. A few also focused on conflicts within villages themselves.
PARTICIPATORY MAPPING, PLANNING AND MONITORING

One of the most important ways communities can gain effective control over their traditional territories is to get together to take stock of what they have, how they have been using it, and decide what they want to do going forward. That leaves them much better positioned to make claims and negotiate with governments, companies, international funders, and NGOs, address unwanted incursions, and implement other collective activities.

This goes way beyond drawing lines on a piece of paper or preparing formal documents. It is a chance to work collectively, see their surroundings in a new light, learn from each other, reaffirm their identity, and build consensus. It is especially important to capture the unique knowledge and perspectives of elders, women, youth, and minority groups.

Sometimes participatory maps and plans are an initial step towards obtaining formal land rights, public funding, or permits to harvest forest resources. Other communities use them for their internal purposes or to negotiate with neighbours or companies.

This is a major area of many of our partners’ work and they do it well. Besides overseeing participatory processes, about one dozen have formal Geographical Information Systems or work with other organisations who do.

By far, the largest mapping undertaking in 2023 was that of the BRWA ancestral domain registration agency in Indonesia. It oversaw a massive effort to mobilise tens of thousands of villagers to map millions of hectares, and then verified, registered, and certified those maps, and worked to get them recognised by government.

children.text: BOX 12: THE POWER OF MAPS
lands in 2023. This represents an area nearly the size of Costa Rica.

In most nations where the Tenure Facility works, government maps do not delineate the territories stewarded since time immemorial by Indigenous Peoples and local communities. This is why a critical step in the fight to protect the communities from land grabs – is literally putting these them on the map.

BRWA’s work involves much more than holding a GPS device and marking down the coordinates of a boundary. For BRWA, mapping is a holistic, participatory process. It involves all the community walking the forest and navigating rivers – often with women, youth, and men together. It can be empowering for the entire community. “These maps we make are ours”, one Indonesian community member said. “We decide what maps to make, and what use we will make of them – whether it’s a map we keep to ourselves because we do not want to share with outsiders where our valuable bird nest caves or sacred sites are, or whether we make a map to negotiate with a government agency to get our land and forest tenure secure and recognised.”

Indonesia’s BRWA is the largest independent agency in the world helping communities map and register their territories. With its sister organisation, the AMAN indigenous alliance, it has mobilised tens of thousands of villagers to support community-led mapping processes across the Indonesian archipelago.
With Tenure Facility support, BRWA registered, verified, or certified 4.9 million hectares of indigenous community

body.text: Two other cases that stand out when it comes to mapping were forest management group. One is CAGDFT’s work in the DRC to assist 10 communities seeking government recognition of Local Community Forest Concessions to map 246,018 hectares The other is that of Nepal forest group, FECOFUN, the Green Foundation Nepal, and Indigenous Peoples researchers CIPRED, which together supported 382 Community Forest User Groups and indigenous communities to map the forest use, rights, and governance in 45,198 hectares of forest.

Besides mapping, communities often take additional action. Many projects are for their own use, but others are required by governments to grant titles or certificates, allow them to log or collect forest products, or give them funds. In the DRC, Guatemala, and Nepal, communities prepared forest management plans. In Guyana, they made Village Sustainability plans to access carbon funding, wildlife and watershed management plans, and strategic plans for District Councils. In Panama, COONAPIP helped territories to prepare Land Use Plans, Sustainable Resource Use and Community Development Plans, and Environmental Management Plans. Together, the planning efforts involved tens of thousands of people, and millions of hectares.

Six Latin American partners organised and trained villagers to use drones and patrol their forests on foot to monitor forest fires, illegal logging, mining, hunting, and land invasions. At least two tested water quality for pollution. This was especially successful in Bolivia, Guatemala, and Peru, where communities successfully monitored over one million hectares. In Guatemala, local monitoring has all but eliminated forest fires in the community forest concessions, although they continue to pose huge threats in neighbouring national parks.
PROTECTED AREAS

A surprising share of the projects involved community rights in and around protected areas. In Bolivia, Brazil, Guatemala, Panama, and Peru, and mostly recently the DRC, partners helped to create large biological corridors by ensuring that communities, which respect and protect the forests, have secure rights to areas next to parks. In Brazil and Peru, COIAB and indigenous human rights and environmental group FENAMAD supported reserves for uncontacted and recently contacted Indigenous Peoples. Indigenous local governments such as the South Rupuni District Council in Guyana and the two recently recognised Autonomous indigenous Governments in the Multiethnic indigenous Territory and Cavineño territories took on responsibilities for conservation in their territories and where protected areas overlap. Indigenous confederations CONAIE and CONFENAIE in Ecuador and COONAPIP in Panama worked to title indigenous lands in protected areas, while respecting their integrity.

Partners in the DRC and Kenya addressed evictions of indigenous villagers from parks and ensuing conflicts. And, as noted previously, Guatemala’s community forest concessions manage large areas forest sustainably in the multiple use zone of the Maya Biosphere Reserve in the Petén.

Building trust between communities and parks authorities can take time. But, when successful, rights-based conservation leads to better outcomes. Indigenous elders can impart their knowledge to community youth, who then use new geo-spatial and information technologies to alert parks authorities so they can jointly control illegal incursions. This kind if of collaboration is needed to keep the forest standing, revitalise indigenous culture and traditional knowledge about conservation, and build confidence along the way.

Three new projects in 2023 focused largely on ensuring indigenous and community rights and improving relations between communities and park authorities in large blocks of tropical rainforests, where indigenous and other forest communities overlap with protected areas. These included the CREF network and Tropenbos DRC projects in North Kivu in the DRC and the FENAMAD Project in the Madre de Dios River Basin in Peru.

Even though these projects only recently began, the CREF Network, which aims to conserve and restore forests, has already reached agreement with the director of the Maiko National Park in North Kivu to collaborate on community participation in park demarcation and development of its new 10-year management plan. This has opened a path for potential comanagement with communities of approximately one million hectares adjacent to the park.

For much of the year, CONAIE and CONFENAIE worked diligently with Ecuador’s Ministry of Environment to design a mechanism for titling the estimated one million hectares of indigenous lands in protected areas in the Amazon. However, these efforts stalled with the country’s change of government, and the some of the action shifted to the courts.

Similarly, Panama was progressing on titling indigenous lands in protected areas in the Province of Darien when a competing claim from the Sinclair Oil Company appeared unexpectedly. COONAPIP ultimately convinced the government to discard this claim, but it significant delayed the process.
