body.text: Nixon Andy, Coordinator of the ”Guardia” of the A’i Cofan Protected Community of Sinagoe, Ecuador.
GENDER EQUALITY

Full equality between men and women and respect for women’s rights is not only fair and just, but essential for Indigenous Peoples and other communities to sustain control over their traditional lands and deliver the benefits that offers. Tenure Facility takes that seriously and it is a key tenet of every project we fund.

Two new projects last year focused entirely on women’s rights to use forest resources (the MIQCB babassu coconut breakers project in Brazil, and the UEFA project in the eastern DRC) and three others had predominantly women leadership (the CNAMIB project in Bolivia and Podáali and CONAQ projects in Brazil.)
Almost all of the 23 projects that were fully operational last year reported achievements related to gender equality. For example,

items.text: Thanks to PCN, Colombia awarded the first ever collective land title to an Afrodescendant women’s group.
Indonesia’s National Land Agency increased land registration for women and youth in agrarian reform sites in collaboration with KPA.
Indigenous women leaders in Peru prepared and presented a proposal to the Ministry of Agriculture to enhance women’s involvement in land titling.
TenForest in Burkina Faso organised a dozen workshops and a radio campaign to get local governments and landowners to make land available for women’s groups, with great success.

Some 61,788 rural women joined the Forest Rights Committees decision-making bodies in Odisha, India, at least in part due to efforts by eco-group Vashundhara to ensuer that the committees followed government policies requiring at least half of their members to be women.
Local governments in Nepal approved Local Forest Acts requiring that at least half the elected leaders of Community Forest User Groups be women.

body.text: Partners established new women’s commissions and/or ensured women were better represented in their governing bodies. They conducted studies to assess women’s problems, needs, and concerns, and they adopted gender policies and strategies and hired gender experts. They trained thousands of women and men about women’s rights, gender bias, and genderbased violence. Many supported women-run forestry, farming, and handicrafts enterprises. They helped women’s groups get legal status, develop experience in fund management, mobilize support from male leaders, and connect with other women’s groups.

children.text: BOX 13: WOMEN’S WAY

It is no coincidence that half the leaders elected in the newly recognised indigenous autonomous self-governments in Bolivia were women. Indigenous women’s confederation CNAMIB, the main project partner, has nurtured women whose main mission is to protect resources for their children and grandchildren, to take on broader decision-making roles and in ways that mend bridges and avoid polarisation. CNAMIB is developing a woman’s leadership and governance training programme, along the lines of some other partners. These programmes not only enable women to participate in decisions about their forests and territories, but also gives them legal tools they can use, if they wish, to counter machismo, femicide, and gender violence.

Women get together to cook, wash laundry, gather medicinal plants, exchange knowledge about how to make juice from the majo palm nut (Oenocarpus bataua) and jewellery from the flaming red and black sirari seeds (Ormosia coccinea). In these gatherings they decide what rights they want recognised to their forest farm gardens where their food and fruits grow, and to the places with plants producing valuable weaving fibres and seeds. Women’s gatherings are also places of comfort and solidarity when women suffer harassment or worse in their homes and communities, or physical violence from illegal loggers. They provide spaces where women can discuss the need for bi-lingual education and inter-cultural health systems as integral parts of their tenure security in their own languages.

body.text: Many mixed-gender partner organisations have focused on gender-parity policies, ensuring 50/50 women/men representation.

In all projects women are stepping up and out in front of village gatherings and discussions with government, elected as leaders in their organisations, participating in mapping activities, joining their territory’s environment defender network.
PARTNERSHIP WITH GOVERNMENT AND COMPANIES
Practically all our partners collaborate with some national or local government agencies. Typically, both sides achieve much more together than they could on their own.

The organisations we work with inform government policies and procedures, prepare paperwork so governments can process claims, train government officials, design apps and other software used by government agencies, facilitate government visits to communities, provide intelligence about illegal activities, fund events co-sponsored by governments and government travel to meetings. Ironically, when there is great political instability, constant rotation of agency staff, and chronic loss of public documents, our indigenous, Afro-descendant, and community partners often serve as the State’s institutional memory.

At the same time, our partners depend on government to issue titles and certificates, approve land use and management plans, remove illegal occupants, protect local leaders, regulate extractive activities, and collect data.

Some collaborations are mandated by law, such as the negotiations between Indigenous Peoples and government agencies in Colombia’s National Commission of indigenous Territories (CNTI) and Amazon Regional Roundtable (MRA) and Indonesia’s joint KPA and Ministry of Agrarian Affairs Task Force to Define Priority Agrarian Reform Areas. In response to growing tensions between Indigenous Peoples and government, Panama’s president established a National High-Level Roundtable for government ministers and COONAPIP to regularly meet to address indigenous land titling, illegal land invasions, deforestation, and other sensitive issues.
In at least eight countries. our partners signed formal Memorandum of Understanding with government agencies, delineating specific activities and each partiers’ rights, and responsibilities.

children.text: BOX 14: WHERE THERE IS A WILL, THERE IS A WAY: AN UNUSUAL PARTNERSHIP IN COLOMBIA BEARS FRUIT
gave US$ 50,000 to our Afro-descendant community partner PCN to purchase it and lend it to the land agency.

When new authorities came into office recently in Colombia and Brazil, they were shocked by the sorry state of the public land agencies they inherited. Years of neglect had left a legacy of few trained staff, shoddy equipment, and poorly kept records.

PCN and ANT signed a formal agreement stipulating that they would jointly determine how and where to use the equipment. PCN members would visit the Afro-Colombian communities to explain the process and arrange logistics for the ANT surveyors. Then the surveyors would come with PCN staff and make the maps.

Juan Camilo Cabezas, who had headed the Ethnic Affairs department of Colombia’s National Land Agency (ANT) for some time, was a competent public servant, who always wanted to help Afro-descendant and indigenous communities get their titles, but he never got much support. Once the Colombian administration changed in 2022, he came under great pressure from above and below to move faster. But Camilo Cabezas was seriously shorthanded.

The results exceeded everyone’s expectations. In a matter of months, they mapped 12 Afro-Colombian and five indigenous territories covering 82,236 hectares and another 63 parcels with 16,198 hectares, which the government purchased to redistribute to communities. ANT was able to straighten out many of their records. Most importantly, this built trust and good will between the ANT, PCN, and the communities. Eighty percent of the land titled by ANT in Afro-Colombian communities last year was in territories they worked in together with the PCN.

So, he reached out to Tenure Facility asking for equipment to survey land for titles and tablets to help sort out records. Seeing the evident sincerity, we were inclined to help. But rather than giving the equipment directly to the government, we

body.text: These partnerships cover a range of issues, including land titling, forest concessions and management plans, indigenous self-government, conflict resolution, protected areas, watershed management, deforestation, forest fires, carbon finance, spatial data, legislative and regulatory reforms, and consultations about public and private investment projects. In some cases, the indigenous communities and NGOs pay the bill; in others the government, and in others they agree to split it.

There were fewer partnerships with companies, but those that existed were quite successful. In India business school ISB organised roundtables and meetings with key industry leaders to show them how secure forest tenure and sustainable supply chains can help achieve business goals. This opened the way for successful partnerships and large purchase agreements between women’s groups and private companies in Jharkhand (plant-based food group AAK for sal seeds used for oil and medicines (Shorea robusta)), and Odisha (paper company BGPPL for bamboo), and progress on several others.

The Julian Cho Society and the Toledo Alcaldes Association in Belize persuaded the government to approve a protocol requiring government agencies and project developers to get the consent from Mayan communities before making investments there. That allowed communities to reach more favourable agreements with solar power companies and a telecommunications project, with other negotiations underway.
USE OF THE COURTS
Sometimes partners need to take governments to court to get them to act. About half of our projects reported using national or international courts successfully last year. For example:

items.text: Ecuador: A court ordered the Ministry of Environment to provide the Siekopi Indigenous People with the first ever certificate of indigenous land ownership inside a protected area.

Nepal: The Constitutional Court issued a temporary stay stopping the triple taxation by the federal, provincial, and local governments of the 22,000 Community Forest User Groups, a system that has hindered their development.
Guyana: In response to a legal ruling by the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, the government revoked 17 mining licenses.
Belize: To comply with a 2015 Caribbean Court of Justice Consent Order, which required the government to title Mayan community lands, the government presented a draft Maya Customary Land Policy.
Kenya: Local courts issued interim orders to stop evictions of Ogiek villagers by the Kenyan Forest Service from their ancestral lands in the Mau Forest.

Ecuador: In a separate case, the Constitutional Court struck down a law governing consultations of Indigenous Peoples about mining concessions, temporarily halting new concessions in indigenous communities.

body.text: In other cases, partners provided legal assistance to community leaders who have been criminalised by the justice system for lawful efforts to defend their territorial rights or to seek protection from threats of violence.
STRENGTHENING ORGANISATIONS AND FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT

As we saw in Section 2, Tenure Facility funds mostly large and well-organised indigenous and community groups. Typically, they have greater reach, political legitimacy, credibility, and track records serving communities and protecting forests, than most other civil society groups. This belies the common notion that they are weak and lack “capacity”.

Even so, because most rely heavily on local staff and volunteers, have had limited access to funding and use traditional forms of organisation, these groups often lack relevant professional skills, tools, and systems. Tenure Facility funding allows them to explore which of these might help, and which might distract and distance them from their constituents.

The influx of Tenure Facility funds in 2023 allowed many organisations to expand, prepare strategic plans, develop human resource policies, train staff and members, improve communications, upgrade Geographic Information Systems and data bases about land claims and community forests, and hold organisational congresses and assemblies. Partners organised most of the training themselves, but in a few cases Tenure Facility funded outside groups (e.g., If Not Us than Who for filmmaking, Spring Strategies for finance, Cadasta for Geographic Information Systems, Landesa for gender equality, and Tebtebba for preparing proposals to the Green Climate Fund.) These efforts will help partners to sustain their expanded efforts once Tenure Facility funding ends.

More than a dozen projects we funded reported major improvements in financial management. Since most partners are not used to managing projects that large, we have given special attention to this aspect. That begins with rigorous financial due diligence during project design, which helps to identify and agree on areas for improvement.

In addition to technical assistance by our finance team and training by local service providers, we funded Spring Strategies to pilot a customised, highly interactive, in-person and virtual, training programme on Financial Innovation and Resilience for partners’ managers and finance staff. Forty-five of them from Kenya, Nepal, India, Guyana, and Belize participated in the initial pilot, which will be replicated in other regions.

Many partners adopted new budgeting, procurement, cash management, bank reconciliation and audit policies and procedures. They hired administrative staff, arranged additional training, and acquired accounting software. This allowed two partners in eastern DRC, who initially lacked the capacity to manage a large grant, to meet all their agreed milestones and administer funds themselves. Similar advances allowed indigenous federation FENAMAD to manage its own project in Peru, after being a sub-grantee in a previous project.
In at least three instances, partners reported that improving their financial management allowed them to increase and diversify their funding from other sources.

While financial management may sound routine and potentially boring, the reality where our partners apply them is anything but. This is often in remote areas, with few or no banks, and limited internet, where they must haul cash over dirt roads and treacherous rivers, and risk assault along the way. They must also find ways to transfer funds to local groups that have not been registered as legal entities and/or have no bank account acceptable to the auditors.

One innovative mechanism that has been gaining traction to address such situations are so-called community “territorial funds” – funds created by the indigenous and community organisations themselves to channel money and other assistance to local groups in the territories.

The four projects we funded in Brazil last year all involved territorial funds. In the case of the indigenous Podáali Fund, Tenure Facility staff were able to help them to improve their financial and grant-management systems, based partly on systems developed by the Samdhana Institute in Indonesia. Samdhana is the fiscal sponsor for the FOKER network in west Papua, which is applying similar systems.

children.text: BOX 15: AN INNOVATION – COMMUNITY TERRITORIAL FUNDS

Ever since the US$ 1.7 billion Glasgow climate summit pledge by funders in 2021 to support indigenous and community land and forest rights and management, there has been an outpouring of interest in how to fund those groups directly.
DEMA fund is almost two decades old, but most funds are just getting started.

Tenure Facility will support four territorial funds in the region. Two already exist, the indigenous Podáali Fund, an independent entity created by COIAB, and the Babassu Fund, which is as a unit within the babassu coconut breakers organisation. Two others, the National Quilombola Fund and Mizizi Dudu Quilombola Fund, linked to CONAQ and its local affiliate Malunga, in the State of Para, are being incubate with the support of the Brazilian Fund for Human Rights and the DEMA Fund.

This coincided with a flurry of interest in a novel funding approach called territorial or community funds. Indigenous, Afrodescendant, and community federations are creating specialised units that provide small grants for land and forest rights, local institutions, and other homegrown initiatives. These funds get the money where it needs to go. Just as importantly, they give diverse groups within communities the opportunity to discuss and decide who to fund for what and to monitor the process.

Good ideas tend to spread, and this one has caught on like wildfire. Indigenous and community organisations recently set up funds in Mesoamerica and Indonesia, and the Global Alliance of Territorial Communities created a global platform called Shandia to promote them.
The Brazilian Amazon is ground zero for territorial funds. It has almost a dozen, with another six under construction. The

body.text: CHALLENGES
Of course, 2023 was not all a bed of roses. Political changes, insecurity, and natural disasters threatened progress in a handful of countries. In others, problems emerged in the projects themselves.

Partners in Burkina and Mali faced Jihadist insurgencies, while in the eastern DRC and Colombia, it was armed militias and criminal gangs. Political unrest in Panama and Peru became a distraction and made it hard to travel for meetings and events, as protesters and police blocked the roads. Ecuador’s unexpected snap elections interrupted fruitful negotiations about titling indigenous territories in protected areas and forced them to practically start again from scratch. Strong Congressional opposition to indigenous and Afro-descendant land rights threatened the mandate of the new Ministry of Indigenous Peoples and the budgets of key agencies in Brazil, putting the Lula Administration on the defensive.

Despite a unanimous ruling by the African Court for Human and People’s rights ratifying the rights of the indigenous Ogieks to live in their ancestral lands in the Mau Forest, Kenya’s Forest Service violently evicted hundreds of villagers from their homes. Governments in Belize and Guyana shunned dialogue with autonomous indigenous groups and insisted on acting unilaterally.
A major earthquake affected the work in Nepal; in Guatemala, the problem was floods.

Elections of new leaders in indigenous organisations FENAMAD in Peru and OPIAC in Colombia slowed things down, while they settled into their new roles. In several countries tensions between partners had to be resolved. It also proved difficult to find appropriate options to help partners manage funds in the eastern DRC.
TRAINING, EXCHANGES, AND OUTREACH

The main way Tenure Facility achieves impact beyond funding large projects and building ties between our partners and other stakeholders is to facilitate sharing of practical knowledge that partners, government officials, funders, and others can use. It is also working with others to make the cause for greater and more appropriate types of funding for efforts such as those described above.
One main form this took in 2023 was a half dozen large multistakeholder in-person and virtual Learning Exchanges focused on specific topics, with over 1,500 participants.

body.text: Our work and our partners’ combined efforts helped to sustain momentum for greater funding for communal tenure rights and forest guardianship, following the US$ 1.7 billion pledge in 2021.

Together with the Rights and Resources Initiative (RRI), we co-hosted the Path to Scale coalition, an informal network of 50 public and private funders and intermediaries committed to scaling-up global funding to legally recognise the land and resource rights of Indigenous Peoples, local communities, and Afrodescendant Peoples, particularly women. Path to Scale forms the nucleus of a vibrant community of organisations that share information and experiences about funding trends and proven best practices and implement joint events and initiatives.

We also supported indigenous and community platforms to promote their owners’ visions and mechanisms for channelling funds. We contributed to the Global Alliance of Territorial Communities’ Shandia platform, which promotes and facilitates direct, long-term, effective, and sustainable funding for their constituencies. We accompanied AMAN and KPA as they launched the Nusantara fund in Indonesia and supported the Network of Indigenous and Local Populations for the Sustainable Management of Forest Ecosystems in Central Africa (REPALEAC) to follow-up on a Central African and Congo Basin forum of indigenous and community women it had cohosted with RRI. We contributed to an Asia Indigenous Peoples Pact workshop in Indonesia to design a new Indigenous Peoples of Asia Solidarity Fund (IPAS) and we funded Tebtebba Foundation to help Indigenous Peoples organisations access funding from the Green Climate Fund.

Our staff met regularly with funders and made presentations about our experiences and the importance of this funding at events organised by the European Union, World Bank, Ted Audacious, Skoll Foundation, Bridgespan, Childrens Investment Fund Foundation, Ford Foundation, UNFCCC High Level Champions, Devex, Conservation International, and Avina Foundation, among others.
LESSONS LEARNED

The differences in context between regions (and sub-regions within them) are too great to use the same approach everywhere. Our approaches must vary depending on the level of political will and space for civil society, the institutional configuration of government decision-making and policy implementation, the types, and capacities of partners available to work with, and the presence and influence of various types of donors. This has increasingly led us to organise our programmatic work around regional – and in some cases sub-regional teams (i.e., Congo Basin, Amazon Basin).

children.text: BOX 17: LESSONS LEARNED ABOUT PARTNERSHIPS

Women’s collectives and womenled organisations represented a quarter of our partners in 2023. Their achievements further demonstrate their unique power to broker agreements, resolve entrenched conflicts, and design solutions that result in more equitable outcomes.
As we expanded the number and complexity of our partnerships in 2023, we learned a great deal, and some key lessons emerged:

items.text: Democratic decision-making within our partner organisations can be slow, but there is a lot to be said for democracy. Democratically elected indigenous or community leaders have greater credibility, legitimacy, political influence, and capacity to mobilise community resources. They are more responsive to their constituents.

More than half of our projects involve multiple partners. Multi-party projects require the partner organisations to create clear, formal governance arrangements from the outset. They tend to work best when the organisations have already worked together for some time and there is trust-based history for collaboration.

To achieve lasting change not just for men but for everyone in the communities, we need to redouble our support for women’s collectives (e.g., CNAMIB, MIQCB, UEFA) and organisations led by women (e.g., AMAN, KPA, CONAQ, APA, Podáali).

children.text: We discovered the wisdom of the African proverb: “If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together.

body.text: The level of support from government agencies tends to fluctuate over time. When discussing potential projects with partners it is useful to consider different scenarios in that regard and how they might adjust. In general, in cases where central government agencies have become less supportive it has been necessary to consider approaches that communities can undertake on their own, options involving sub-national governments and alternative national government agencies and using international agencies and other stakeholders to communicate with and influence government officials.

Our partners have a growing demand for reliable accurate and understandable information and advice about carbon markets. We must find ways to respond to that demand, often by identifying experts and reference materials to which we can refer partners.

As threats to the Amazon continue to mount, alliances between those committed to its conservation and indigenous organisations are of increasing importance. Although there is a history of conflict and mistrust between indigenous and Afro-descendant groups and some conservationfocused organisations, when they join forces, major wins can be achieved. The fight against rollbacks for protection of Uncontacted Indigenous Peoples in Voluntary Isolation in Peru mentioned above is one example, as was the landmark referendum in Ecuador last August that banned oil drilling in Yasuní National Park.

We learnt that we must pay more attention to disability. In many communities where we work, mercury contamination of waterways used for daily fishing, drinking, and washing has unleashed a growing epidemic of disabilities.

Within the Tenure Facility team itself, we are also challenged to understand how so-called disabilities are perceived or valued within our partners’ cultures. One culture may view as impediments and mock, for example, cognitive changes linked to aging, but other see it as the wisdom of elders. Blindness and abilities to wander into other dimensions are often hallmarks of spiritual leaders and shamans, held in high regard, and cared for. These are dimensions we must explore with humility.
SECTION 5:
How?
Our Special Sauce

Tenure Facility is not just any financial mechanism. It specialises in providing funds, managing grants, and facilitating partnerships in ways that are specifically suited to the needs of indigenous, Afro-descendant, and community organisations and the contexts in which they work. These are ways that are “fit for purpose.”
We have not got all the mix of ingredients right in our sauce, but here are some parts of the recipe which worked well this year.

Our first few partners came to us through longstanding relationships they had with the Rights and Resources Initiative, an organisation that incubated and spun off the Tenure Facility. It had identified them as effective and well-respected organisations in their countries, and had built trust with them over years, often before any funding.

In 2021, we adopted a more formal system, to identify countries with good opportunities and the best strategies and potential partners for promoting indigenous, Afrodescendant, and community land and forest rights. This includes a combination of gathering information, analysing and having dialogues with potential partners. The feedback from partners was that this worked reasonably well in 2023.

We first do an initial “scan” of a country and, if things look promising, follow up with thorough country assessments, which give a good sense of who we might work with on what. In countries where we already work and know the relevant players well, we sometimes skip this step and open dialogues with potential partners.

Our board then approves our country assessments and initial project ideas to make sure they fit our strategies and principles. Having diverse board members that come from the constituencies we serve helps to make us more accountable. In 2023, we completed country assessments in Gabon and Republic of Congo (Brazzaville), and began others in Cambodia, Lao PDR, and Vietnam.

Prospective partners design the projects themselves, which are usually centred on their on-going work related to land and forest rights, territorial governance, gender equality, and youth inclusion. Once they have an agreed concept note and we have looked at their financial management, we sign a five-year partnership agreement and fund them to prepare a full proposal and consult with the communities involved to make sure the proposed project has their free, prior, and informed consent. We visit the partners physically, face to face in their offices, and in some of the forests and territories where participating communities plan to carry out activities – in the jargon of some funders – “project appraisal”. But we prefer to see this as the beginnings of “accompaniment”.

If a group does solid work and has strong support from their constituencies, they can still manage a project even if they are not so experienced at administering funds. In that case, they need to find a fiscal sponsor to administer their funds, but they still call the shots. In 2023 about one third of the projects had fiscal sponsors of various sorts. In some cases, this was temporary, until the group could manage its own funds.

Practically all the projects include activities to help partners strengthen their organisations. Most of these activities are proposed by the organisations themselves to meet their own objectives, although a small part of that may be to help them do a few things they need to do to manage a larger Tenure Facility supported project. Most partners are keen on those as well, because they are already looking how to scale up their efforts.
We listened to what our partners have had to say on all of this.

As a result, this year we continued to make the process and requirements of agreeing grants as clear and simple as possible, including simpler ways for partners to share with us what results they are achieving with Tenure Facility grants.

We have adapted our financial assurance or due diligence requirements to types of organisation and contexts. Our partners are not monolithic organisations. They work at multiple levels, have varying governance and organisational structures, and speak multiple languages.

Working in remote forest regions, far from capital cities, such as in eastern DRC, brings challenges – few or unstable banks, few or no roads, long distances, weak digital connections, few people giving receipts for goods and service. In 2023, we commissioned a study on best practices for cash management in settings like these to be finished next year. We will also learn from the experiences of community territorial funds as to how they successful operate in such conditions.

We take care to respect our partners’ sovereignty over the data, maps, stories, and photos they produce. To get the details of that right, in 2022 we commissioned a study on the issue and then last year held a workshop with academics and partners to discuss the results. That allowed us to have a clearer understanding with partners on this, and with our donors.
With What?
OUR BOARD OF DIRECTORS

The Board of Directors in 2023 was composed of 12 senior international leaders and experts from 11 countries, who all serve in their individual capacities. They include Indigenous leaders, development practitioners, human rights experts, and finance specialists. For full biography see: https://thetenurefacility.org/aboutus/our-team/board-of-directors/

Myrna Cunningham, the Board Chair, is a Miskitu leader from Nicaragua. She is a medical doctor, former chair of the United National Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, founder ex-rector of the University of the Autonomous Regions of the Caribbean Coast of Nicaragua (URACCAN), and former Governor of Nicaragua’s northern Caribbean Region. She has chaired many international boards and received numerous international awards and honorary degrees.

Augusta Molnar from the United States served most of 2023 as Board Secretary and head of the Project Selection Committee. She previously serves as Director of Country and Regional Programmes at the Rights and Resources Initiative (RRI) and Senior Natural Resource Specialist at the World Bank and Chairwoman of the Board of the Mountain Institute.

Similarly, Donald Roberts was Board Treasurer for most of the year. Don is the President & CEO of Nawitka Capital Advisors and has over 30 years’ experience as a financial services executive, investment bankers, and equity professional.

Other Board Members included: Eleni Kyrou, a Greek economist who chairs the governance committee and is Senior Impact Programme Officer at the European Investment Bank (EIB); Abdon Nababan, former Secretary General of AMAN in Indonesia; Joan Carling, a Kankanaey sociologist from the Philippines, who serves as Executive Director of Indigenous Peoples Rights International (IPRI); Juan Martinez, a Mexican anthropologist working at the Inter-American Development Bank (ADB); Albert K. Barume, a Congolese Lawyer who works as an expert on Mali at the United Nations Security Council (UNSC); Christine Halvorson, a Brazilian anthropologist who is currently Programme Director at Rainforest Foundation United States (RFUS); Carl Lindgren is a Swedish Independent Economic Advisor; and Mary Hobley is a British forestry expert, author, and development practitioner.
Tenure Facility’s Executive Director, a human rights lawyer from the Philippines, also serves as an ex officio member of the Board.

In 2023, the Board also welcomed three new members who formally joined in 2024: Viviana Figueroa, an Omaguaca lawyer specialized in Indigenous Peoples rights from Argentina; Lesle Jansen, a South African lawyer who serves as Head of Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities Resource Rights at Jamma International, and Jennifer Tauli Corpuz, a Kankanaey-Igorot lawyer from the Philippines, who is the Managing Director for Policy at Nia Tero.
OUR TEAM
At the end of 2023 the Tenure Facility team was composed of 56 people from diverse backgrounds, half of whom work part-time. (So, an equivalent of 40 full-time people.)

We are organised in four units (programme, finance, operations, and development) plus the Executive Director’s team. Within the programme unit, there is a sub-unit focused on Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning. Three regional teams, each with its own regional coordinators, draw on members from these four units.

In 2023 Tenure Facility continued its trend of decentralising its programme team to the regions where our projects are based. During the year we had team members in one dozen countries (up from eight in 2022): six in Latin America, four in Africa, and two in Asia. This allowed us to be close to partners in the key countries where we worked and intimately knowledgeable about their diverse cultures and political economies.

During the year, we created a new Development Unit, responsible for donor relations and communications, and brought on a new lead for Strategic Partnerships, several new financial controllers, and a new specialist in Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning.

We also contracted Arthan human capital advisory services in India to help refine our global Human Resources policy to ensure that we have the diverse talent that we need, policies are clear and comply with relevant legislation, and all team members are treated fairly and well.

What Next?

As we looked back over the year, many things inspired us. The new generation of powerful women leaders outperforming their predecessors and taking what’s rightfully theirs. Seeing scientists and so-called experts finally catch up with what Indigenous Peoples have known forever – that they are the Guardians of the forests. The hunger among many philanthropies to find new ways to relate with people that they fund. The growing list of relevant governments willing to embark upon this journey. Local leaders who put their lives on the line every day, but never cease to smile.

It has been hard to imagine and internalize how quickly Tenure Facility has grown and matured, from an idea in a few peoples’ heads to an international organisation with extensive networks and a formidable footprint on the ground. But even more amazing, the best is yet to come.

Almost half of our projects are just taking off and the earlier projects are coming of age. This year we just began new projects in Cameroon, Peru, and both Congos, and we expect to launch several more. We may not be able to double again the area and number of communities where we see advances this year. But we are confident that our recent investments will yield high returns.

More formal land and forest rights. Organised and vocal communities. Mapping and monitoring. People and parks. Women and girls. New and surprising partnerships, with unusual suspects. And greater global desire to learn and assist.

The up-coming Biodiversity Summit (COP 16) in Colombia in October 2024 and the Climate Summit in Brazil (COP 30) are especially propitious. Indigenous Peoples, Afro-descendants, and communities will be high on the forest and climate agenda. With strong social movements mobilizing to make their voice heard and governments willing to listen. They offer moments to reflect on the progress since the US$ 1.7 billion pledge of support for this agenda and to lay out a framework for moving forward.
No. The challenges won’t go away. The dark clouds continue to haunt us, and breaking news distracts. We know that there will be setbacks.

We also know that while the task could not be more urgent, it won’t happen overnight. These are inherently lengthy processes. Forests don’t grow in a day. It takes time to talk things through, and bureaucracies are not known for speed. While we count and savour each short-term victory, this will take a little patience.

Nor do we naively believe there are simple solutions to these very complex problems. But we do believe that those closest to the problems that affect them are best able to develop the solutions that work. Since they have taken up the mantle, we are there to lend a hand.
And, so far, it seems to be working.
When we report back next year, we hope you will be with us again. It means a lot to us, and the world around us. And it might just surprise you.
